blocks.content: November. He was at the desk again — but different this time. She could tell by the way he was sitting.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: What are you working on.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Something I've been thinking about for a while.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Good something or hard something.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Both. I think both.
She brought two cups and sat on the arm of the couch nearby.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You want to talk about it.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Not yet. Not until I know if it's real.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Okay.
She didn't push. She just stayed close.
December. Sunday night. Kit at the stove, squinting at a sauce label with the reading glasses he kept insisting he didn't need.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: How many teaspoons is a dash.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: That's not a unit of measurement.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: It says dash on the label.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Give me those.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I can read it.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You are holding it at arm's length.
He laughed. Real laughter — the kind that caught him off guard.
She looked at him. He looked at her. Something loosened in the room.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: There you are.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Here I am.
